verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blc_implement_user_registration
verdicts.reasoning: The registration handler is intentionally nopriv because it serves the front-end registration form for unauthenticated visitors — this is by design. Crucially, it verifies a WordPress nonce ('blocksy-register') before doing any work (lines 193-203). An unauthenticated attacker cannot obtain that nonce without first rendering the registration form page, and the nonce is tied to the session. Additionally, registration only proceeds if the site admin has enabled user registration (get_option('users_can_register')) or WooCommerce my-account registration; both are gated at get_registration_strategy() (line 171). The handler delegates actual account creation to WordPress core's register_new_user() or WooCommerce's wc_create_new_customer(), both of which enforce their own validation. No privilege escalation or bypass is possible: newly created users receive the default subscriber role. One minor concern: the WooCommerce path at line 232-237 checks $_POST['role'] === 'seller' and auto-logs in the customer with wc_set_customer_auth_cookie(), but this only elevates to WooCommerce customer/seller role, not WordPress admin.
verdicts.exploitation_path: None exploitable without a valid nonce. Even if registration is open, attackers can only create subscriber-level accounts — identical to the normal self-registration flow. The 'seller' role escalation requires WooCommerce + Dokan and only grants vendor-level access.

verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blc_implement_user_login
verdicts.reasoning: The login handler is a thin wrapper that includes wp-login.php via ob_start() and then relays the login result as JSON. All actual credential validation is performed by WordPress core. The handler adds a login_redirect filter to capture the redirect URL and send it as JSON on success, then sends back the rendered login page HTML on failure. There is no nonce check in implement_user_login() itself, but this is not a vulnerability: the function does not perform any authentication bypass — it delegates entirely to WordPress core's wp_signon() which enforces rate limiting (via wp_login_failed hooks) and brute-force protection from security plugins. An attacker learns nothing from this endpoint that they could not learn from wp-login.php directly. This endpoint is a UI integration shim, not an authentication bypass.
verdicts.exploitation_path: No exploitation path. Credential brute-forcing is identical in difficulty to attacking wp-login.php directly. No information disclosure beyond what core login already provides.

verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blc_implement_user_lostpassword
verdicts.reasoning: The lost password handler verifies a nonce ('blocksy-lostpassword') before proceeding (lines 76-86). An unauthenticated attacker cannot forge this nonce without first loading the front-end page that renders the form. The handler delegates to WordPress core's retrieve_password() or WooCommerce's retrieve_password(), both of which send a reset link to the registered email address — the attacker cannot reset a password without access to the victim's email inbox. The nonce check is the gating control; without it, the handler returns wp_send_json_error(). No account takeover is possible.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Requires valid nonce obtainable only from the front-end form page. Even with a valid nonce, a password reset email is sent to the account owner's email; the attacker gains nothing without inbox access.

verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blocksy_companion_load_cookies_consent_scripts
verdicts.reasoning: The load_cookies_consent_scripts handler reads the list of scripts to load via an apply_filters() call and returns them as JSON. It accepts no user input, performs no database writes, and serves public configuration data needed by the cookies consent popup for unauthenticated site visitors. Being nopriv is intentional and required for the feature to work. There is no injection surface and no sensitive data exposure.
verdicts.exploitation_path: None. This is a read-only public endpoint serving configuration data.

verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blc_newsletter_subscribe_process_ajax_subscribe
verdicts.reasoning: The newsletter subscribe handler is intentionally public (newsletter signups are for unauthenticated visitors). The handler accepts EMAIL, FNAME, GROUP, and DOUBLE_OPTIN parameters and sanitizes them (sanitize_email, sanitize_text_field). No nonce is verified — phpcs:ignore comments explicitly acknowledge this. The lack of nonce verification means CSRF-triggered subscriptions are possible (an attacker could trick a logged-in user's browser to subscribe an email address), but the real risk is that the endpoint can be used to spam/flood a third-party newsletter service with arbitrary email addresses. Rate limiting is entirely absent at the plugin layer. The $manager->subscribe_form() call delegates to a configured newsletter provider (Mailchimp, etc.) which may impose its own limits. The GROUP parameter, if passed to the provider without validation against a whitelist, could allow subscribing to arbitrary mailing lists/groups. Without seeing the Provider class implementation, the severity depends on what the provider library does with the group value.
verdicts.exploitation_path: An attacker can POST arbitrary email addresses to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=blc_newsletter_subscribe_process_ajax_subscribe without authentication. This enables: (1) email address harvesting confirmation — each subscription attempt reveals whether the provider accepted it; (2) mass subscription of victims' email addresses to mailing lists (email bombing/harassment); (3) potential subscription to unintended lists if GROUP is not validated against a whitelist.
verdicts.cvss_estimate: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:L/A:N ~ 5.3 (Medium) — but limited real-world impact depends on newsletter provider

verdicts.title: Nopriv AJAX: blocksy_get_trending_posts reflecting page param
verdicts.reasoning: The automated scanner flagged this as an XSS via the page parameter. However, the page parameter is immediately cast to int via intval(sanitize_text_field(wp_unslash($_REQUEST['page']))) on line 18. The integer value is only used as a pagination argument in a WP_Query call. The response is JSON (wp_send_json_success) containing post data fetched from the database — title, URL, image HTML, and taxonomy HTML. These are rendered via WordPress's own output functions (get_the_title, get_permalink, blocksy_media, blocksy_html_tag with esc_url). No user-supplied data is reflected in the response. The 'page' parameter itself is never echoed back. This is a false positive.
verdicts.exploitation_path: None. Integer cast eliminates any injection via page parameter. All response data is sourced from the WordPress database and escaped on output.

verdicts.title: maybe_unserialize on widget option_value in widgets-import.php
verdicts.reasoning: The maybe_unserialize call at line 213 operates on $row->option_value retrieved from wp_options for existing widget data ('widget_' . $id_base). This is reading data that is already stored in the WordPress database — it is not deserializing attacker-controlled input from the network. The data originates from the site's own wp_options table. Furthermore, this code path is only reachable via the demo install flow, which is guarded by current_user_can('edit_theme_options') at line 29 of the import() method. The widget data passed to import_data() comes from a server-fetched demo package (not directly from user POST), and it is pre-processed via json_decode(wp_json_encode($widget), true) on line 161 before being inserted, converting it to a plain PHP array — so the actual widget data being stored never contains serialized PHP objects from attacker input. The maybe_unserialize here is reading pre-existing site data to determine the next widget slot number.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Requires edit_theme_options capability (administrator). The data being unserialized is from the site's own database, not from attacker-controlled network input. Not externally exploitable.
verdicts.cvss_estimate: N/A — requires admin, operates on own-database data

verdicts.title: maybe_unserialize on option_value in helpers.php:231
verdicts.reasoning: blocksy_companion_get_option_from_db() at line 214 reads an option from the wp_options table by name and calls maybe_unserialize() on its value. The option name is passed by the calling code (blocksy_companion_get_capabilities()->accounts_cache reads 'fs_accounts'). The value is data previously written to the database by the Freemius SDK during plugin activation/licensing — it is not sourced from any HTTP request parameter. maybe_unserialize() is the standard WordPress pattern for reading options that may be serialized. Since the data is not attacker-controlled at the point of deserialization (it was written by trusted code during a privileged operation), there is no deserialization attack surface here. This is a standard WordPress pattern, not a vulnerability.
verdicts.exploitation_path: None reachable from an unauthenticated HTTP request. The option value is set by Freemius SDK licensing code during administrator actions.

verdicts.title: maybe_unserialize on meta_value in helpers.php:268
verdicts.reasoning: blocksy_companion_get_network_option_from_db() at line 239 reads from wp_sitemeta and calls maybe_unserialize() on the meta_value. This is only used on multisite installations to read the 'fs_accounts' network option set by Freemius SDK. Same analysis as obje-004: this is not user-input-controlled at the point of deserialization, and the pattern is standard WordPress.
verdicts.exploitation_path: None. Network option value is written by the Freemius licensing SDK during admin-initiated plugin activation.

verdicts.title: unserialize() in capabilities.php:247 without allowed_classes
verdicts.reasoning: The casttoclass() private method at line 246 uses unserialize() after using preg_replace to swap the class name in a serialized string. The regex replaces the class name portion of an 'O:...' serialized object with stdClass. The input to this function comes from $this->accounts_cache which is the 'fs_accounts' WordPress option set by the Freemius SDK — trusted, administrator-written data. While unserialize() without allowed_classes is a code quality concern, the actual data being deserialized is not attacker-controlled via any HTTP request. The regex transformation is also limited: it only rewrites the outer class name and only handles a serialized object — it does not handle nested objects. A PHP object injection attack would require an attacker to control the wp_options 'fs_accounts' value, which requires database write access (typically requiring compromised database credentials or administrator-level WordPress access). If an attacker already has database write access, they have far simpler attack paths. The lack of allowed_classes parameter is a best-practice violation but not an externally exploitable vulnerability in context.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Would require controlling the 'fs_accounts' wp_options value (database write access or WP admin). If an attacker can write arbitrary options, they have already fully compromised the site. Not exploitable from unauthenticated HTTP requests.
verdicts.cvss_estimate: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:H/PR:H/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H ~ 6.6 (Medium) — only in the context of a secondary chained attack with prior database access

verdicts.title: SQL injection in content-export.php: post IDs in raw WHERE clause
verdicts.reasoning: Line 533 constructs: $where = 'WHERE ID IN (' . join(',', $next_posts) . ')'. The $next_posts array comes from array_splice($post_ids, 0, 20) where $post_ids is fetched from the database on line 144 via: $wpdb->get_col('SELECT ID FROM {$wpdb->posts} $join WHERE $where'). The $post_ids array therefore contains only integer post IDs retrieved from the WordPress database itself — they are not sourced from any HTTP request parameter. The $args array (line 70) uses hardcoded $defaults — there is no HTTP input flowing into these arguments. The $join and $where clause in the initial query are built from validated/prepared values (post type from whitelist, dates via strtotime, author via intval-equivalent prepare %d). This is essentially WordPress's own export_wp() function copy/pasted, and the post ID array is generated from a parameterized query result. The concatenation of integer IDs from a database query into a subsequent query is not exploitable via HTTP requests. Furthermore, the entire export functionality requires current_user_can('edit_theme_options') as enforced in demo-export.php:11.
verdicts.exploitation_path: Requires edit_theme_options (administrator). The values concatenated into SQL are integer post IDs sourced from a previous parameterized SELECT — not from HTTP request input. Not exploitable from the network.
verdicts.cvss_estimate: N/A — requires admin, data is from own database query result

summary.most_dangerous_finding: blocksy-companion-nopriv-005 (newsletter subscribe endpoint allows unauthenticated mass-subscription of arbitrary email addresses to configured mailing lists without rate limiting or nonce verification, enabling email harassment/bombing of victims)
notes: All three account-auth nopriv handlers (registration, login, lostpassword) are protected by nonce verification and delegate to WordPress core — not account takeover vectors.
The unserialize() in capabilities.php (obje-006) operates on Freemius SDK licensing data from wp_options, not on HTTP request input. It is a code quality concern but not externally exploitable.
The SQL injection in content-export.php is a false positive: the concatenated values are integer post IDs sourced from a prior parameterized database query, not from HTTP parameters.
The maybe_unserialize findings in helpers.php and widgets-import.php all operate on database-resident data, not on attacker-controlled HTTP input.
The newsletter subscribe endpoint is the only finding with a real (if low-severity) external attack surface: absence of rate limiting and nonce check enables email abuse.
